EKS Field Notes · GDS Solutions

How a deploy actually gets from your laptop to a browser

A map of the real path a change takes through Amazon EKS — and the one silent fork in that path that cost the most debugging time: which cluster is kubectl even talking to?

Cluster gds-yaml-cluster Region us-east-1 Workload gds-deployment · 2 replicas

01 — The path from build to browser

Every box below is a real object that exists in your setup right now. The dashed amber branch is the mistake that happens most often — not a broken cluster, but kubectl quietly pointed somewhere else.

Developer machine
docker build · docker push
Docker Hub
sguri003/gds-app : <tag>
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml — routed by whatever kubectl config current-context says
✓ correct context
gds-yaml-cluster
real EKS cluster
✕ wrong context
docker-desktop
local cluster — unrelated
Everything "succeeds" here. Nothing you see was ever live.
EKS CLUSTER · gds-yaml-cluster
Workload — namespace: default
Node Group: gds-workers
2 × t2.medium
Deployment: gds-deployment
rolls out new ReplicaSet per image tag
Pod
Pod
Service: gds-service
type LoadBalancer
Access — namespace: kube-system
kube-system
LB Controller
watches Services
IRSA
IAM Role
AWSLoadBalancerControllerIAMPolicy
AWS Network Load Balancer
provisioned by the controller above
Browser
via the NLB hostname — never a bookmarked IP

02 — The lesson worth remembering

!

Check the context before you check anything else. A wrong kubectl context doesn't error — it just quietly succeeds against the wrong cluster. Every symptom of "the deploy vanished" traced back to this, not to Kubernetes itself.

Run this first, always:

kubectl config current-context

Expect arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:<ID>:cluster/gds-yaml-cluster. If it says docker-desktop instead, fix it with:

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name gds-yaml-cluster --region us-east-1

03 — Glossary, for anyone new to EKS

Cluster
The managed Kubernetes control plane. AWS runs it; you never see or patch the master nodes yourself.
Node Group
The actual EC2 instances that run your containers — here, 2× t2.medium named gds-workers.
Pod
The smallest deployable unit: one running container (or a small group of them sharing network/storage).
Deployment
Declares "I want N copies of this image running" and manages rolling updates when the image changes.
ReplicaSet
Created automatically by a Deployment to keep the desired Pod count running. Each rollout makes a new one and scales the old one to zero.
Service
A stable address that load-balances traffic across matching Pods. Type LoadBalancer asks the cloud to provision a real load balancer for it.
LB Controller
A pod in kube-system that watches for Services/Ingresses and actually provisions the AWS NLB/ALB behind them.
IRSA
IAM Roles for Service Accounts — lets a Kubernetes ServiceAccount assume a real AWS IAM role, so pods can call AWS APIs with no hardcoded credentials.
kubectl context
Tells your local kubectl which cluster it's talking to. Can silently point at a local cluster instead of EKS — the single most common source of "it's not working" here.
gds-yaml-cluster · us-east-1 · companion reference to the deployment SOP document
